speaker: interviewer
text: So you were telling me before we broke on the last tape, before we took a break, that you, you ran away from hearing the Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you hid in a cemetery until--

speaker: interviewee
text: On a cemetery. Then it was so dark. I went-- I remembered where, where my late husband said that he's hiding, so-- and I knew the people, too, because I went-- their children went to school, younger than I, but I knew them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I tried to go over there. And I went. And I knocked on the door. She let me in, Mrs. Hacken. Hacken. Maria. Maria Hacken. Hocker?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hacken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hacken.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a remarkable woman. And she took me in, and you know, and I told her the story where we ran, what happened. She said, don't worry. You'll find out in the morning. Well, I didn't know where my father is. And then she said, you know, I don't know if you know him. But Moshe Resnick and his brother-- no. Moshe Resnick is here. And then early in the morning, I'll take you to the barn. And you will sit with him, and during the day, we'll find out about my father. I didn't tell her, because she told my husband not tell anybody that she's hiding Jews. She did it for a mitzvah. And she didn't want anybody to know. Anyhow, in the-- early in the morning, she took me to the, to the barn. And I-- and Moshe was there. And I-- we talked. And during the day, she sent her son to that-- it was her niece to find out my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he found out that my father was someplace else. But he's back there. And that night, I was supposed to go back to my father. In the meantime, my late husband and his brother were hiding by this woman, by these, by these people, the Hackens.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they had made a double attic, you know, a wall in the attic. And that's where they hide. That I didn't know. But Moshe told me. So at night when she told I had to leave, so they said come up to the attic. And my brother-in-law, my late brother-in-law said, listen. And that, that was the worst time. There was no place where to hide. That was of the summer of the fall of the summer of '43. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] OK. Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Shall I continue? Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, so my brother-in-law said, listen. A non-Jewish person is saving us. We cannot let you go. You'll stay with us here. They had a very good hiding place. And then I don't know. I don't remember how my father came. Anyhow, my father came also. My father didn't stay with us. He ran away. He came in between. Only this was done also after midnight, always. And I was there quite a few, few months. And once it was quiet, and we were talking. And I, I was laughing something. And his son heard my voice, the son of the, of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Maria's.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maria's son. And he told his mother. And she came back from the, from the-- I don't know-- wherever she was. And she called my husband down. And she told him, how dare you do that thing like this?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm saving you, and you're going behind my back? And she told him we all have to leave. So I went down to her in the barn. And I start crying. And I asked her, listen. I'm going to leave. But don't-- let the Resnicks stay.

speaker: interviewee
text: They don't know anybody here. They don't know where to go. And I was [INAUDIBLE]. And she said, OK. Stay also. And she let me stay. But meantime, my father used to come also. My father, she never found out that he was with us. Then again, the Ukrainian came. The, the main office was there. And, and, oh, yeah, another story I have. And while we were sitting in that double--

speaker: interviewer
text: Attic?

speaker: interviewee
text: --attic, my husband didn't have no [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what it means? He couldn't like-- first of all, he wanted to hear news. And secondly, he liked to visit Jews. There was, you know-- he found out where they are. Because she never told anybody that she hides Jews. The others did tell her. So she told my husband. So he used to go at night and visit the Jews. One night he went also. And after midnight time, so 2 o'clock in the morning, he's coming back. And in the meantime, the Ukrainian, the partisan, [NON-ENGLISH] as we called him, came in. And the main office is here by Hacken, because that was the richest house in, in that village. And they had a patrol outside. And they caught my husband, the patrol, and bring him in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she somehow heard it. And she says, when they brought him in, oh, you drunk. Where are you? That's my brother-in-law, the drunk. Where are you so late?

speaker: interviewee
text: And [INAUDIBLE]. And they took it for granted that's her brother-in-law. And then when, when it was quiet, she brought him out to the barn. He came up to us. We saw right away that something-- he was half dead. They would have shot him on, on the spot if she would have-- she saved him. And life was going on like that. My father was coming. Then it came nearer to the end. My father also came and stayed with us because it was-- we heard or read that the German are near. And in fact, the, the front where they fight came already to our village. And the main stop, the main office, again, by-- and it took maybe two, three weeks, they were there. And in that time, we were four upstairs. And she didn't miss-- for two people, she-- for three of us, she always brought us food. But we shared it for four of us. And my father used to cough. And we used to, oh, get so panicky. But thank god, everything went smoothly. Finally, the Germans left, and the Russian came in. So when the Russian came in, this good woman didn't let us go out during the day. We had to wait until it got dark. And then we left her place. She did it just for mitzvah. She didn't want nobody-- she told us she doesn't want anybody to know that she was hiding us. And then the Russian came in. I guess that's the end of it. It wasn't easy, either, with the Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the Russians came in, you came out of hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We came out of hiding. And we went with them. They took us to the city, to Lutsk, to the big town. But we didn't know. But they had arrested us. They didn't believe that we Jews. They thought that we are, we are spying, spying on them. And they arrested us. And they brought us to police. Early in the morning, they-- all kind of questions they asked. Anyhow, in the morning, they arrested-- they mobilized all the men. We were 13 Jews, between them, two women and a boy of 14, 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- us three they let go. But the men, they drafted to the army, all of them, my father, my late husband, and his brother. And there were other people. Here I go out. And I don't have where to go. I don't know anybody. So they took your father away to the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So but meantime, they still kept them in the police station there. So my father went to the head of the state police station and asked him, please, let-- give me a permit to go out just for a few hours to find for my daughter where to stay. So my father came out. And as soon as he went on the, on the streets, he found-- we met a Polish man and his wife. You see, the Ukrainian were so bad, they were killing Jews and were killing later Polish people, everybody. They wanted to be themselves only. So the Polish people also from the farms, left and came to the city. They were afraid to be on the farms.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this farmer, the Polish, took me in. I think his wife was also Czech. So I had already worked to stay. Do you remember their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at the moment. No. So I had where to stay. But then I didn't have no food. What to do? So my father went with me, went looking for a job. So I went to, to a restaurant. And I got a job at the restaurant. And so I got to raise something what to eat. And my father went back. And they took them away all to the, to the army. They took them to Russia. And I stayed in Lutsk, worked, worked very hard. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: So during that time that your father and your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Late husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: --were away, did you hear all the stories about what had happened to, to the remaining Jews of Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure we heard. We, we knew already. We saw what's happening to us from, from out of town, how many people, just a few people of us survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that you had seen that grave, that mass grave.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. After I was in Lutsk, it was a-- the war, war was going on, but we were freed already. One day myself, my cousin, a few, three or four people of us, yeah, we got a, a policeman was, or an army man, and he took us to our Sienkiewiczowka, the little town. So that's where we saw the grave. And I went to the post office. At the post office, I found letters from Jews that escaped when the Russian came in, when the German took over between German and Russian. And they were made to Russia. And some of them survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were writing letters home. I collected all those letters. And I wrote to everybody. And I did connect two brothers and a sister. They are still-- one of them has died already in Israel and-- What's their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Landow. They got connected.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it feel when you went back?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was horrible. For me, it was horrible. I was, again, alone. My father was away. My husband wasn't my husband yet. But my boyfriend, let's say, was away. It was very hard. And then you had to work for survival.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your father come back?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father came-- my husband came first. I had-- when I went to this village, Sienkiewiczowka, I knew some of my uncle and aunts put in my parents some of, of, like, good clothes, some jewelry, nice coats, and clothes they left with them before we went into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I ran into some. And some of them gave me back the clothes. They were very nice people. So my-- I was already in contact with my husband. My husband wanted to come back from the army. He did all kind of things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he got a permit. I send him money. I sold some-- a coat, I think. I send him money. And he did come back to Lutsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got married when?

speaker: interviewee
text: In December, 18 of '44, because we were freed April of '44. And the men were drafted. So until December, I was alone. I met my cousin. So we stayed together. It was a struggle. Then my husband came.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your cousin's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lerner.

speaker: interviewer
text: First name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's a mother and a daughter, Lisa and Fania. They live in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did your father eventually come back?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father came when we were already in Germany. And I had already a child of six, six weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got married. And once the war had ended, you moved to--

speaker: interviewee
text: When we got-- when my husband came back, we got married. And then when you come on a, on a, you know-- he had a, a paper that he can come back. But with this paper, because he was a soldier, had to go in, in an office to register that he's [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went in. And they arrested him. They said, this is not a legal paper. It's wrong. Maybe it was not legal. Who knows? And they arrested him. They put him in a room.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, it was already-- it was almost close to-- it was in March. And April was the end of war. So my-- there was an organization that Polish citizens can go back to Poland. My husband was a Polish citizen. Somehow he knocked out the window. And he escaped from there, ran out. And he went straight to this office. And he got the papers right away. Luckily, he got a paper that he is a Polish citizen. He can move to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he came where I was working. He said, you must leave right away. We have to leave right away. Other way, they'll draft me again. And the next day in the morning, we left for Poland. And then from Poland, wherever we stopped, I used to write letters to my father, and my husband, to his brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in order to tell them where we are going, to my father I used to write, I'm going to see your cousin. He had-- my father had a cousin by the same name, Benjamin Walter, in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- they understood that we are on the way to Israel. Through Germany, they knew that-- so while the war-- when the war end, we were in Poland. We were-- where were we? In Lodz, I think. And then we went, not legally.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that we meant that we are going to Israel. But we went to the American zone. And we were in the DP camps. It was a lot till we get there, too. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: In, in Germany, where did you go in Germany, the name of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was a DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a DP camp. Yeah. We stayed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the DP camp, my oldest son.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] Jack Resnick.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father then joined you later on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, six weeks after-- my son was six weeks old. He came. He was in Munich. And somehow he heard. And my husband went to Munich and brought him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was that reunion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, what should I tell you? Unbelievable. And my father, my father was a very young man. And how old was he? I was 23 when he was born. 44? Young man. And he-- every time he lost a child-- and every time he had to call my name, he called my mother's name. He was like reliving. And my son, he used to call by his youngest son's name. I used to just let it go. I never stopped him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what were the circumstances behind moving to America? You moved to, to New York City, was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- yeah. We moved to New York City.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came in May of '49.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was it that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in the DP camp. And my mother had two sisters. My father had a sister here in America. Now we wanted to go to Israel. But it was impossible legally to go. And then this cousin of my father's wrote to us. Don't. If you have a chance to go to America, go, because it was bad times, you know, in Israel, in '47, '48, the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the sisters in America? What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had a sister by name Fanny Kremnitzer. And my mother had two sisters, Sara Reimer and Fanny Reimer. They all dead now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they helped you come over.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes. Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once you were in America, what did your husband do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: What he didn't do. [LAUGHS] He started-- he had to, you see-- in order to come here, my aunt, they weren't rich people. They had to-- well, they signed that they'll let us live with them. But then you had to have a place of work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father had cousins. And they had shops of clothing. So they signed. And they did give my father and my husband. Second day or the third day of when we came to America, they took the train and went to work in Manhattan. Eventually, your husband did what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he started working as a operator, you know, a sewing machine. My father, a presser. But he didn't work too long. He wanted always business for himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: He went in-- and-- what kind of? In decorating business. You know, like at that time, it was modern slipcovers for the furniture. And he had bought a set. And he wanted to make-- I had a small child-- to make a set of covers. So he bought material. And he made it. And it fitted beautiful. So [INAUDIBLE] he's a cutter. He started working as a cutter and then [INAUDIBLE]. Then he went into this business. And then he lost it. Then we were in grocery business.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a long story. Finally, he was in as a job in textile.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's where he retired from. I worked. I helped my husband in the groceries where he was. And then I worked as a teller, as a bank teller for about almost 25 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: In New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York, in Borough Park, in Brooklyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father died when?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father died in May 28 of '66.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of heart--

speaker: interviewee
text: Heart attack. Heat attack.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you ended up having another son?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is Mark.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many grandchildren today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have five grandsons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Five grandsons.

speaker: interviewee
text: No girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joshua, Seth, Mickah, Jed, and Zachary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what do you want Joshua, Seth, Jed, Mickah, and Zachary to learn from the--

speaker: interviewee
text: From my experience?

speaker: interviewer
text: From your experience. They're going to watch this tape. What do you want it to tell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: To learn to be brave. Fight for your rights. In a free country, they can do it. [SOBS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a hard question. Is there anything else you want to tell me today?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you'll ask me something else, I'll tell you, I don't remember anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know why, why it is-- why, why do you think that you and your father-- why did you survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? This is a question very hard to answer because I don't know the answer. Just plain luck, if you're a believer, [NON-ENGLISH]. But it's plain luck. There was no-- not because I knew better or you know, he knew better. No. It's just plain luck that we survived. And maybe it was [NON-ENGLISH] that we should have a new generation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my husband, Moe Tetelbaum. We got married two years ago on November 30. But we knew each other. We had met the first time in DP camp in Germany in [NON-ENGLISH]. And through the years, we were friends. We knew our spouses. And my husband lost his wife 10 years ago. My husband was dead five years ago. So about four years ago when my husband heard that I'm alone, he called me. And that's how our romance started. [LAUGHS] Finally, we got married. My husband has a son, Barry, who lives in Las Vegas right now in America. This is my father and his second wife. My father's name was Benjamin Walter. His wife's name was [NON-ENGLISH]. His wife was an Auschwitz survivor. My father died in May 28, 1966.

speaker: interviewer
text: This picture was taken when?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture must have been taken, I would say in the '50s, '55.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In, in upstate New York. I can see it. On vacation. This is a picture of, of my late husband and myself. The picture was taken in, in about '84. We were honored by the UJA Federation in New York-- to raise money, naturally.

speaker: interviewer
text: Name? His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my late husband's name was Maurice Resnick. This is a picture of my oldest son, Jack, and his family. Jack, next to him is his wife, Terry. And next to Terry is Seth, which is now 18. And in the middle, this is Joshua. That was his past bar mitzvah. And these are the twins. At that time, they were like 2 and 1/2 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Names, Mickah and Jed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Picture was taken when?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In December 1987 in New York. It was Joshua's bar mitzvah. This is my youngest son, Mark, and his son, Zachary. Zachary's 11 1/2 years. He's very, very adorable. And they live in Los Angeles. My son is an accountant by profession. But he works as a-- forgot-- something in the Kaiser company. These are my two sons as they look now. On the left is Jack, which is a doctor. And the younger one is Mark, an accountant. And I'm the proud mother. [LAUGHS]
